project.name: cortexdj
project.description: AI-powered EEG classifier that detects emotional states during music listening and curates Spotify playlists grounded in what your brain actually did — mapping each 4-second EEG segment into four emotion quadrants (relaxed/calm/excited/stressed) through an agentic FastAPI + Next.js stack.

nodes.summary: Loads the pretrained CBraMod EEG backbone and probes its output feature dimension via a dummy forward pass.

nodes.name: symmetric_info_nce
nodes.summary: Symmetric soft-target InfoNCE contrastive loss over EEG and audio embeddings, treating same-trial pairs as positives in both directions.

nodes.name: retrieval_metrics
nodes.summary: Computes Recall@k and mean reciprocal rank for EEG-to-audio retrieval by ranking cosine similarities against ground-truth trial pairings.

nodes.name: encode_session
nodes.summary: Encodes a session's EEG segments through the CLAP-aligned encoder and mean-pools them to a single normalized embedding vector.

nodes.name: load_audio_waveform
nodes.summary: Loads an m4a stimulus file as a mono waveform resampled to the CLAP model's expected sample rate.

nodes.name: EegCLAPEncoder
nodes.summary: EEG encoder pairing a CBraMod backbone with a SimCLR-style projection head, projecting EEG into the shared CLAP embedding space.

nodes.name: ClapAudioEncoder
nodes.summary: Wrapper around HuggingFace CLAP that embeds audio waveforms into the shared audio-text space for contrastive training.

nodes.name: contrastive_dataset.py
nodes.summary: Dataset layer for contrastive training: DEAP EEG windowing, stimulus audio resolution, a persisted CLAP audio-embedding cache, and the paired EEG/audio dataset.

nodes.name: trial_to_eeg_windows
nodes.summary: Slices a DEAP trial into fixed-length EEG windows, resampling each to the backbone's expected segment length. Shared slicer reused across the contrastive pipeline.

nodes.name: build_audio_embedding_cache
nodes.summary: Resolves stimulus audio, embeds it with the CLAP encoder, and persists a trial-id to embedding cache on disk, reusing it on subsequent runs.

nodes.name: _resolve_audio_path
nodes.summary: Resolves a recorded stimulus path to an existing on-disk audio file, falling back to the shared audio cache directory when the recorded path is absent.

nodes.name: DeapClapPairDataset
nodes.summary: PyTorch dataset yielding paired EEG windows and precomputed CLAP audio embeddings per DEAP trial for contrastive training.

nodes.name: contrastive_train.py
nodes.summary: Training driver for the contrastive EEG-CLAP encoder with SequentialLR warmup+cosine scheduling, gradient accumulation, TensorBoard logging, and best-checkpoint saving.

nodes.name: train
nodes.summary: Orchestrates contrastive EEG-CLAP training: builds datasets, runs warmup+cosine scheduling with grad accumulation and early stopping, and saves the best checkpoint plus metadata.

nodes.name: _train_one_epoch
nodes.summary: Runs one contrastive training epoch with gradient accumulation, gradient clipping, and per-phase timing instrumentation.

nodes.name: _evaluate
nodes.summary: Evaluates the contrastive model on a loader, computing InfoNCE loss and retrieval metrics without gradients.

nodes.name: main
nodes.summary: CLI entry point parsing contrastive-training arguments into a ContrastiveConfig and invoking train.

nodes.name: ContrastiveConfig
nodes.summary: Dataclass of contrastive-training hyperparameters: epochs, per-group learning rates, weight decay, warmup, patience, and gradient accumulation.

nodes.name: dataset.py
nodes.summary: DEAP dataset loaders producing differential-entropy features or raw EEG windows with per-subject label binarization and on-disk caching.

nodes.name: scores_to_quadrant
nodes.summary: Maps continuous arousal/valence scores to one of four Russell affect-space quadrant labels.

nodes.name: load_deap_participant
nodes.summary: Loads a single DEAP participant's preprocessed EEG signals and label arrays from a .dat pickle file.

nodes.name: _compute_label_thresholds
nodes.summary: Computes per-subject arousal/valence binarization thresholds (subject median or fixed-5) used to derive binary labels.

nodes.name: load_dataset
nodes.summary: Factory that builds either a differential-entropy feature dataset or a raw-EEG dataset for the requested training mode.

nodes.name: DEAPFeatureDataset
nodes.summary: Cached PyTorch dataset producing differential-entropy features and binary arousal/valence labels per DEAP segment for the EEGNet pipeline.

nodes.name: DEAPRawDataset
nodes.summary: Cached PyTorch dataset producing raw multi-channel EEG windows and binary labels for the CBraMod pretrained pipeline.

nodes.name: metrics.py
nodes.summary: Pure classification metric functions (per-class recall, macro-F1, balanced accuracy, class weights) plus a majority-class baseline predictor.

nodes.name: macro_f1
nodes.summary: Computes macro-averaged F1 across classes from true and predicted labels.

nodes.name: per_class_recall
nodes.summary: Computes recall for each class from confusion counts over predictions.

nodes.name: balanced_accuracy
nodes.summary: Computes balanced accuracy as the mean of per-class recalls.

nodes.name: class_weights_from_labels
nodes.summary: Derives inverse-frequency class weights from a label distribution for class-balanced cross-entropy loss.

nodes.name: MajorityBaselinePredictor
nodes.summary: Trivial baseline classifier that always predicts the training-set majority class, used as a reference floor.

nodes.summary: Defines the EEGNet-inspired dual-head classifier predicting binary arousal and valence from differential-entropy features.

nodes.name: EEGNetClassifier
nodes.summary: EEGNet-inspired dual-head network with temporal/spatial/separable convolutions predicting binary arousal and valence from DE features.

nodes.name: predict.py
nodes.summary: Inference layer loading a trained dual-head checkpoint and predicting arousal/valence scores, affect state, and band powers per EEG segment.

nodes.summary: Loads an EEGNet or CBraMod dual-head checkpoint and returns an inference-ready model for the configured backend.

nodes.summary: Extracts the flattened differential-entropy feature vector (channels x bands) consumed by the EEGNet classifier.

nodes.name: .dockerignore

nodes.name: Dockerfile

nodes.languageNotes: Uses BuildKit cache + bind mounts (--mount=type=cache/bind on uv.lock and pyproject.toml) to resolve dependencies without adding them to a layer, and multi-stage inheritance where both local and hosted derive FROM a shared app runtime base.

nodes.name: local

nodes.name: hosted
nodes.summary: Production image stage that bakes src/ into the image (no bind mount available) and launches the app via run_hosted.sh.

nodes.name: ci.yml

tour.title: What Is CortexDJ?
tour.description: Start here to understand the mission: CortexDJ classifies EEG into four emotion quadrants (relaxed/calm/excited/stressed) for every 4-second window, then curates Spotify playlists grounded in what your brain actually did. The README lays out the seven headline features and the tech stack, while the demo-workflow catalog walks through the end-to-end presenter flows — brain-derived recommendations, cross-session comparison, and contrastive track retrieval — that the rest of this tour will show you in code.

tour.title: Backend Entry Point
tour.description: The FastAPI application is where the backend boots. `app.py` wires CORS, request-context and logging middleware, and a lifespan handler that loads the EEG model into memory once at startup so every request can reuse it. `main.py` is the top-level router that aggregates and mounts every domain router (agent, sessions, threads, health, retrieval, spotify-auth) into that app — think of it as the switchboard the next steps flow through.
tour.languageLesson: FastAPI's `lifespan` async context manager runs setup code before the server accepts traffic and teardown after it stops. CortexDJ uses it to load the heavy PyTorch EEG model exactly once, avoiding a cold-start penalty on every request.

tour.title: The Frontend Chat Surface
tour.description: Now flip to where a user actually interacts. The chat page mints a fresh thread id per visit and renders an empty `Chat`, and `chat.tsx` is the orchestrator that wires the Vercel AI SDK `useChat` hook to the backend's SSE stream, the active brain context, and thread-cache invalidation. This is the origin of every request the backend answers — the tour now traces one message from here all the way down.
tour.languageLesson: React's `useChat` hook (from @ai-sdk/react) owns message state and streaming. It POSTs to an endpoint, consumes a Server-Sent Events stream, and re-renders incrementally as tokens and tool calls arrive — no manual fetch/state plumbing required.

tour.title: Crossing the Frontend/Backend Boundary
tour.description: The `useChat` hook from Step 3 posts to a Next.js route handler that acts as a thin proxy, streaming the SSE body straight through to the FastAPI backend's agent endpoint. On the backend side, `agent.py` receives that request: it builds the run input, loads the thread's stored brain context, runs the Pydantic AI brain agent, persists messages, and kicks off async title generation. This is the seam where the two halves of the monorepo meet.

tour.title: The Brain Agent and Its Capabilities
tour.description: This is the heart of the system. `brain_agent.py` constructs a Pydantic AI Agent that composes five capability groups (session, insight, playlist, retrieval, classification) plus history compaction and error-recovery hooks under a detailed EEG/Spotify system prompt. `deps.py` defines `AgentDeps`, the dependency container injected into every tool call — carrying the DB session, EEG model, Spotify client, thread id, and active brain context — and the classification capability dynamically injects that brain context into the system prompt so the assistant always knows the user's current mood.
tour.languageLesson: Pydantic AI capabilities use `get_instructions()` to inject dynamic system-prompt fragments at run time. Rather than a static prompt, the ClassificationCapability reads the thread's brain context from AgentDeps and folds it into the instructions on every turn — dependency injection meets prompt engineering.

tour.title: The Agent's Tools
tour.description: Capabilities group tools; this is where the tools themselves live. The session tools list recorded EEG sessions with emotion summaries and return a detailed per-segment analysis of one session, while the retrieval tool embeds a session's EEG into the joint EEG-audio CLAP space and returns top-k matching Spotify tracks. Note the deliberate exception convention: most tools let errors propagate to the recovery hook, but the retrieval tool catches `DeapFileMissingError` inline to give the user a clear, actionable message.

tour.title: Domain Services: Retrieval and Spotify
tour.description: The tools from Step 6 stay thin and delegate real work to the services layer. The retrieval service encodes a session into CLAP space and runs a pgvector cosine search to return the most similar indexed tracks — the engine behind brain-state track retrieval. The Spotify service handles app/user client construction, OAuth token refresh, paginated search, id resolution, and playlist creation, turning the agent's decisions into an actual playlist on the user's account.

tour.title: ML Pipeline: The Quadrant Classifier
tour.description: Underneath the services sits the science. The inference layer loads a trained dual-head checkpoint and predicts arousal/valence scores, affect state, and band powers per 4-second EEG segment. Two interchangeable backends feed it: a CBraMod-backed pretrained transformer with staged freeze/unfreeze finetuning that works on raw EEG, and a lightweight EEGNet-inspired classifier that works on differential-entropy features — selectable by env var and benchmarked leave-one-subject-out on the 32-subject DEAP dataset.
tour.languageLesson: A dual-head model shares one backbone but splits into two output heads — here one for arousal and one for valence. This lets both dimensions of Russell's affect model be learned jointly from the same representation while keeping separate, task-specific classifiers.

tour.title: ML Pipeline: Contrastive EEG-CLAP Retrieval
tour.description: The second, separable pipeline is what makes brain-state track retrieval possible. `contrastive.py` bundles a CBraMod-backed EEG encoder, a CLAP audio encoder, a symmetric InfoNCE loss, retrieval metrics, and session encoding — training both modalities into a shared 512-dimensional embedding space. Once EEG and audio live in the same space, finding the songs that match a brain state (Step 7's retrieval service) reduces to a nearest-neighbor lookup.
tour.languageLesson: Contrastive learning with InfoNCE pulls matched EEG-audio pairs together and pushes mismatched pairs apart in a shared embedding space. Making the loss symmetric (EEG-to-audio and audio-to-EEG) means either modality can query the other — the foundation of cross-modal retrieval.

tour.title: Data and Persistence
tour.description: Everything above is anchored by the async SQLAlchemy models. A shared declarative `base` gives every model its identity; the `EegSegment` model stores each 4-second window's arousal/valence scores, band powers, and features, with classmethods to query and aggregate by session or state; and `TrackAudioEmbedding` is the pgvector-backed retrieval index that makes Step 7's cosine search fast. This layer is the durable memory the agent queries when it looks for tracks that consistently triggered a target mood.
tour.languageLesson: pgvector adds a native vector column type to PostgreSQL and an HNSW index for approximate nearest-neighbor search under cosine distance. Storing the 512-d CLAP embeddings from Step 9 directly in the database means retrieval is a single indexed SQL query, not a separate vector service.

tour.title: Rendering Brain State in the UI
tour.description: Back on the frontend, the streamed tool results come alive as inline panels. `session-visualization.tsx` auto-renders when an `analyze_session` tool call is detected, wrapping the animated emotion trajectory, a recharts arousal/valence timeline, and a shared band-power chart in tabs. `emotion-trajectory.tsx` plots each segment as a point in Russell's valence/arousal plane with a scrubber, and `retrieved-tracks-panel.tsx` renders ranked results beneath retrieval calls with similarity bars, inline preview playback, and Spotify links — closing the loop the tour opened in Step 1.
tour.languageLesson: The trajectory chart uses motion/react's animated `motion.path` with `style={{ pathLength: progress }}` driven by a requestAnimationFrame loop, turning a static SVG path into a scrubbable playback of the brain's journey through affect space over time.

tour.title: Containerization and Orchestration
tour.description: With the application understood end to end, see how it ships. The multi-stage Dockerfile uses a uv-based builder to resolve a locked, no-dev virtualenv, then a slim runtime installs the MNE/scipy system libraries the ML code needs, with local and hosted stages diverging on whether source is bind-mounted or baked in. `docker-compose.yml` orchestrates local development by pairing a pgvector-enabled PostgreSQL service (the home of Step 10's embedding index) with the backend, which waits for a healthy database before starting.
tour.languageLesson: Multi-stage Docker builds use several FROM stages so heavy build tooling (uv, compilers) stays in the builder and never reaches the final image. Compose's `depends_on` with a health-check condition ensures the backend only starts once Postgres reports ready, avoiding startup race conditions.

tour.title: Continuous Integration and Project Manifest
tour.description: The capstone ties quality and tooling together. The GitHub Actions CI workflow runs three parallel jobs on every push and pull request: backend lint/format via pre-commit, backend static typing via mypy --strict, and frontend linting via pnpm. The backend `pyproject.toml` is the source of truth behind it all — declaring the FastAPI/PyTorch/pgvector dependency set, the strict mypy/ruff/pytest dev tooling, and the console-script entry points (train-model, train-contrastive, seed-sessions) that operate the pipelines you toured in Steps 8-10.
tour.languageLesson: GitHub Actions `jobs` run in parallel by default, so lint, strict typing, and frontend checks execute concurrently for faster feedback. Splitting mypy into its own job keeps the fast lint job from being blocked by the slower type-check pass.
